title: Citizens of Heaven
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Citizens of Heaven - Pastor Tito Lyro - Matthew 5_10-12.txt

outline.title: The Sermon on the Mount Is Not a Universal Ethic
subpoints: Thomas Jefferson edited the Bible to keep only the Sermon on the Mount — a common humanist misreading
The Sermon on the Mount is impossible to follow apart from the regenerating work of the Holy Spirit
It is addressed to citizens of the kingdom of heaven, those born again by the Spirit
The Beatitudes describe kingdom citizens, not a program for moral self-improvement

outline.title: The Eighth Beatitude Is Different — and Surprising
subpoints: Unlike the other beatitudes which describe character qualities, this one describes a circumstance: persecution
Jesus shifts from third-person plural ('they') to second-person ('you'), making persecution direct and personal
The blessing matches the first beatitude — possession of the kingdom of heaven — marking an inclusio
For the original Jewish audience this was anti-climactic: they believed only the wicked suffered persecution

outline.title: The World Hates Jesus — and Therefore Hates His People
subpoints: The world hates Jesus; there is no neutral ground (John 15:18-20)
Hatred can fracture even family bonds for Christ's sake (Matthew 10:21-22)
The Christian is not to be surprised by persecution — we follow a crucified Savior
The entire history of the church is told through the suffering of its saints

outline.title: The Blessing Is Not in the Persecution Itself But in What It Produces
subpoints: It is persecution for righteousness' sake specifically that is blessed — not suffering earned by sin or foolishness
Persecution is evidence of genuine faith; when persecuted you walk in the footsteps of the prophets
The reward in heaven far exceeds any earthly suffering (Romans 8:18; 2 Corinthians 4:17)
Paul's own sufferings — beatings, stoning, imprisonment — pale in comparison to heaven's weight of glory

outline.title: Why We Can Rejoice in Persecution
subpoints: Persecution confirms the genuineness of our faith and our union with the persecuted prophets
The reward is not by merit but by grace, and is disproportionately greater than our suffering
Failure to rejoice often reveals attachment to this world and inadequate vision of heaven
Paul's declaration: 'For me to live is Christ and to die is gain' (Philippians 1:21)

outline.title: Why Are We Not Being Persecuted? An Examination
subpoints: Perhaps God is sparing his own glory because our weakness would bring dishonor in persecution
Perhaps our faith is so weak and our conduct so harmless the world has no reason to persecute us
Perhaps pride would cause us to take credit for enduring persecution rather than glorifying Christ
Perhaps we are not peacemakers — and peacemakers are persecuted precisely because they disrupt false peace

summary:
In this sermon on the eighth and final beatitude (Matthew 5:10-12), Pastor Tito Lyro first establishes that the Sermon on the Mount is not a universal ethical code but the description of citizens of the kingdom of heaven — people regenerated by the Holy Spirit. Any attempt to follow the Sermon apart from the new birth is impossible, making the liberal and humanist love for these chapters without foundation. The Beatitudes describe what kingdom citizens look like, not a program for self-improvement.

The eighth beatitude is uniquely structured: it describes a circumstance (persecution) rather than a character quality, shifts from third-person to second-person address ('you will be persecuted'), and repeats the promise of the first beatitude — possession of the kingdom — forming a literary bracket around the entire section. For the original Jewish audience this was anti-climactic, since their theology linked suffering with divine displeasure. Jesus radically overturns this by connecting faithfulness with persecution. The world hates Jesus, and united to him, believers will be hated too (John 15:18-20). The entire history of the church is a history of its suffering saints, from Nero's torches to ISIS beheadings.

Pastor Lyro carefully distinguishes the kind of persecution that is blessed: it must be 'for righteousness' sake,' not suffering deserved through foolishness or sin. This persecution confirms the genuineness of faith, and its reward is not proportional to but far greater than the suffering endured (Romans 8:18; 2 Corinthians 4:17). Inability to rejoice in persecution usually reveals that we are too attached to this present life and have not seriously considered the greatness of heaven.

The sermon closes with honest self-examination: Why are Western Christians experiencing so little persecution? Pastor Lyro offers four possibilities — God may be protecting his own glory from our weakness, our faith may be so mild the world has nothing to oppose, our pride would steal Christ's glory in endurance, or we are simply not peace-makers enough to disturb the world's false peace. The call is to hunger and thirst for righteousness so genuinely that the difference between the church and the world becomes unmistakable.
transcript:
## The Sermon on the Mount Is Not for Everyone

For centuries people from all walks of life have claimed that the Sermon on the Mount provides good principles to live by. Thomas Jefferson, when he edited the New Testament, kept the Sermon on the Mount — he thought it was good moral material for everyone. Some say they do not need the cross or salvation; the Sermon on the Mount is sufficient.

The problem is that the Sermon on the Mount does not work for someone who has not been redeemed by the blood of Jesus Christ. It is impossible to follow if you are not a citizen of the kingdom of heaven. How are you going to be perfect as God is perfect (Matthew 5:48)? How are you going to live without murder and anger (Matthew 5:21–22) apart from the work of the Spirit in your heart? The liberal and humanist love for the Sermon on the Mount is without foundation. This is a sermon for those who have been regenerated by the Holy Spirit.

## A Different Kind of Beatitude

The final Beatitude — *"Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness' sake"* — differs from the others in two significant ways.

First, instead of describing a quality or characteristic of the believer, it describes a *circumstance*: being persecuted. This is unexpected. The kingdom citizen suffers persecution in direct relationship to faithfulness. As Sinclair Ferguson writes in his book on the Sermon on the Mount: we follow a crucified Savior. We should not think it strange if we encounter fiery trials. We are called to be made in his image, and so we will follow in the way of opposition.

Why should we be surprised? Our Lord was called a Samaritan, accused of being demon-possessed, and crucified on trumped-up charges. How then can we be surprised when the world treats his disciples the same way?

Second, this Beatitude shifts from the third person plural to the second person: *you*. Jesus is pointing at each of us: *You* will be persecuted. He makes it intensely personal.

Also noteworthy: this Beatitude repeats the blessing from the first one — "theirs is the kingdom of heaven" — marking it as an *inclusio*, a literary bracket closing this section. The poor in spirit and the persecuted for righteousness share the same promise.

## The World Hates Jesus

We do not live in a friendly place. The Lord himself told us clearly:

> *"Brother will deliver up brother to death, and a father his child; and children will rise up against parents and cause them to be put to death. And you will be hated by all for My name's sake."* (Matthew 10:21–22)

> *"If the world hates you, you know that it hated Me before it hated you. If you were of the world, the world would love its own. Yet because you are not of the world, but I chose you out of the world, therefore the world hates you."* (John 15:18–20)

John the apostle applied this directly to the church in every age:

> *"Do not marvel, my brethren, if the world hates you."* (1 John 3:13)

Let us not pretend the world is our friend. According to our Lord, the world hates him — and it therefore hates us.

## Persecuted *for Righteousness' Sake*

It is critical to keep in mind that the blessing is upon those persecuted *for righteousness' sake*. Not every kind of persecution carries this blessing. Peter is plain:

> *"What credit is it if, when you are beaten for your faults, you take it patiently?"* (1 Peter 2:20)

The blessing belongs to those who suffer for what is right. Paul and Silas in Acts 16 were imprisoned and shackled because they cast out an evil spirit — a righteous act. And there in the dungeon at midnight they sang hymns to the Lord. That is the pattern.

The history of the church can be told through the suffering of the saints. Nero crucified Christians and lit them on fire to illuminate his parties. The Covenanters in Scotland under Charles II were forbidden their own church buildings and worshiped in the open through fall, winter, and spring. In our own lifetimes, Christians in the Middle East have been beheaded for their faith. And the twentieth century was the bloodiest century for Christians in all of history — more believers died for their faith in the 1900s than in all previous centuries combined.

Yet Christ calls these people *blessed, happy, joyful*. He even says to leap for joy.

## How Can We Rejoice in Persecution?

How is rejoicing in persecution possible? Several reasons:

**Persecution indicates the genuineness of our faith.** The level of persecution you face is actually a good measure of the reality of your faith. When you are persecuted for righteousness' sake, you are following in the footsteps of the prophets, who were faithful to God and suffered for it.

**Persecution is followed by great reward in heaven.** This is not a reward of earned wages but of grace. And the reward far exceeds the suffering:

> *"For I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worthy to be compared with the glory which shall be revealed in us."* (Romans 8:18)

The apostle Paul, who was stoned, beaten with rods, flogged, and left for dead because of his faith, calls our present sufferings *light and momentary*:

> *"For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all, while we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen."* (2 Corinthians 4:17–18)

Sometimes we struggle to rejoice in persecution because the reward of heaven seems abstract and distant. If that is the case, it means we have not truly considered what heaven and the presence of Jesus actually are. Paul faced that conundrum head-on:

> *"For to me, to live is Christ, and to die is gain."* (Philippians 1:21)

## Why Are We Not More Persecuted?

You may be asking yourself — as I asked myself while preparing this lesson — why we do not experience more persecution. Let me suggest four possibilities.

**First**, perhaps God is sparing his own glory. If we were to suffer persecution in our current spiritual condition, we might dishonor the name of Christ through our weakness or unfaithfulness. God may be protecting his glory by not allowing us to be placed in that trial.

**Second**, perhaps we are not hungering and thirsting after righteousness enough. If our faith is so weak and our conduct so indistinguishable from the world, why would the world spend the effort to persecute us? We may have become too much like the world for the world to find us threatening.

**Third**, perhaps pride is the obstacle. We may be able to endure persecution physically, but we would take all the credit for persevering through it. God, who opposes the proud, may not be entrusting us with this blessing.

**Fourth**, perhaps we are not peacemakers. Notice that this Beatitude follows immediately after the peacemaker Beatitude. Satan and the world are content to leave undisturbed those who promote a false peace — because they are furthering his cause. Peacemakers for the kingdom, by contrast, will be persecuted precisely because they disturb that counterfeit peace.

The reason we find so little persecution in our experience may simply be that we are not enough for righteousness — not different enough — for the world to notice. As long as we are marginal, as long as we live a form of Christianity that makes no difference to the world, we will not be blessed as those who are persecuted.

But if we are serious about seeking Christ, if we truly hunger and thirst after righteousness, we will be blessed — in this life and in the life to come.
